title: Homer The Whopper

jokes.text: Oh, customers, how I hate them.
jokes.setup: Comic book store owner speaking about his customers
jokes.punchline: Openly admitting he hates the people who keep his business alive
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Comic Book Guy's misanthropy delivered with characteristic bluntness. Sets up his personality immediately.

jokes.text: Excuse me. I just heard that before spider-man was a movie, It was a comic book--is that possible?
jokes.setup: Homer asking about Spider-Man's comic book origins
jokes.punchline: The complete obliviousness to comic book history in a comic book store
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer ignorance joke, well-executed but predictable for the character.

jokes.text: suffering steve ditko! How can you not know that spider-man first appeared in 1962's classic Amazing fantasy #15?!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy's exasperated reaction to Homer's ignorance
jokes.punchline: Using obscure comic creator as an exclamation and citing exact issue number
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - the Steve Ditko exclamation is perfect Comic Book Guy pedantry.

jokes.text: Ooh, 12 cents! I'll take eight.
jokes.setup: Homer seeing the original cover price
jokes.punchline: Thinking he can buy multiple copies at the original 1962 price
jokes.explanation: Good setup/punchline structure showing Homer's complete misunderstanding of collectibles.

jokes.text: What the...? You idiot! Mint condition copies are, like, $40,000 each!
jokes.setup: Homer's naive offer to buy multiple copies cheap
jokes.punchline: The astronomical real value revealing Homer's cluelessness
jokes.explanation: Solid punchline that reveals the gap between Homer's expectations and reality.

jokes.text: Must lie down On pile of unsold hulk hands.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy being overwhelmed by Homer's stupidity
jokes.punchline: Using a pile of failed merchandise as a fainting couch
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual comedy concept - the specificity of 'unsold Hulk hands' makes it memorable and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Hulk smash.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy lying on the pile of Hulk hands
jokes.punchline: The hands apparently responding with Hulk's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Simple callback that extends the previous visual gag without adding much.

jokes.text: everyman? He's not a hero from marvel, dc, Image or wildstorm.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy examining an unknown comic
jokes.punchline: His encyclopedic knowledge noting this character doesn't fit any major publisher
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Comic Book Guy's expertise, though the humor is mild.

jokes.text: Look at that fat guy-- beatin' it like an egg. Yeah, he's scramblinin'' like an egg.
jokes.setup: Criminals watching someone run away
jokes.punchline: Mangling the expression 'beating it' into egg metaphors
jokes.explanation: Weak wordplay that tries too hard to be clever but feels forced.

jokes.text: Egg. Egg.
jokes.setup: The egg metaphor from previous line
jokes.punchline: Continuing to say 'egg' as if it's meaningful
jokes.explanation: Weak escalation that beats the egg joke into the ground without adding value.

jokes.text: Power-absorption power... Absorb!
jokes.setup: Character activating his powers
jokes.punchline: The redundancy of 'power-absorption power' and dramatic delivery
jokes.explanation: Decent parody of superhero dialogue tropes with mild wordplay humor.

jokes.text: put me in sing-sing! It's safer!
jokes.setup: Criminal being confronted by superhero
jokes.punchline: Preferring maximum security prison to facing this hero
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke that plays on the trope of criminals surrendering immediately.

jokes.text: Everyman will stop every crime every time. And I... Am... Everyman.
jokes.setup: Heroic declaration of purpose
jokes.punchline: The overly dramatic reveal and grandiose promise
jokes.explanation: Standard superhero parody that hits the expected beats without surprise.

jokes.text: You made this?! Shut up. No one is interested in the critical judgment of a worthless child...
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy being dismissive of a child's opinion
jokes.punchline: The harsh insult delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy cruelty delivered with characteristic pompousness.

jokes.text: Actually, I like it. You what now?
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy's harsh dismissal
jokes.punchline: Bart's positive opinion stunning Comic Book Guy into confusion
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that subverts Comic Book Guy's expectation and shows his vulnerability to any praise.

jokes.text: Reading it, I escaped from my troubles. Now they're back in spades.
jokes.setup: Bart explaining how the comic affected him
jokes.punchline: The immediate return to reality being worse than before
jokes.explanation: Nice deadpan delivery that captures the temporary nature of escapist entertainment.

jokes.text: Sonofsonofspock, thedorkknight, and ikilledkenny6475.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy listing online critics
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous usernames combining sci-fi references and internet culture
jokes.explanation: Good satire of internet usernames with nice pop culture mashups, especially the Kenny reference.

jokes.text: Don't be afraid of them. They're just lame-o's like yourself. Two of them are me.
jokes.setup: Bart reassuring Comic Book Guy about online critics
jokes.punchline: Comic Book Guy admitting he's two of the three critics
jokes.explanation: Excellent reveal that perfectly captures Comic Book Guy's pathetic online behavior and self-loathing.

jokes.text: Oh, uh, just 335, including the controversial number 289,
jokes.setup: Being asked how many issues he's written
jokes.punchline: The absurdly high number showing his obsessive dedication
jokes.explanation: Good character moment that establishes Comic Book Guy's obsessive nature through specific numbers.

jokes.text: Featuring the death of marmaduke.
jokes.setup: Mentioning a controversial comic issue
jokes.punchline: Killing off the innocent newspaper comic dog
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy mixing the innocent world of newspaper comics with dramatic superhero tropes.

jokes.text: Who knew a troubled person could be creative? Yeah, still waters run deep.
jokes.setup: Learning about Comic Book Guy's extensive writing
jokes.punchline: The naive surprise that troubled people can be creative
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment showing Marge's innocent worldview, though not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Yeah, still waters run deep.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to sound wise
jokes.punchline: Using a cliché that doesn't quite fit while being completely shallow himself
jokes.explanation: Homer trying to sound profound but missing the mark is very in character

jokes.text: I'm so rich.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy after selling his comic rights
jokes.punchline: The simple, understated reaction to sudden wealth
jokes.explanation: Simple understated delivery that works through economy rather than cleverness.

jokes.text: Extension cords. Mixed nuts. Car keys.
jokes.setup: Executives brainstorming movie ideas
jokes.punchline: Increasingly mundane objects as potential film franchises
jokes.explanation: Good satire of Hollywood's desperation for source material, though predictable.

jokes.text: Eh, we've already made everything That could possibly be a movie into a great movie.
jokes.setup: The previous absurd movie pitches
jokes.punchline: The delusional claim that Hollywood has made everything into 'great' movies
jokes.explanation: Good Hollywood satire with nice irony about the quality of adaptations.

jokes.text: Dad, can we go? I've watched all the dvds in the bentley.
jokes.setup: Child being bored at a business meeting
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of having a DVD collection in a luxury car
jokes.explanation: Decent wealth joke that establishes the Hollywood excess through a child's casual comment.

jokes.text: Uh, marv? Ginormous pictures bought the rights To everyman three weeks ago. Damn it!
jokes.setup: Executive getting excited about a new property
jokes.punchline: Learning they already lost the rights to a competitor
jokes.explanation: Solid setup/punchline that captures Hollywood's competitive nature and poor communication.

jokes.text: You'd expect us to believe That a red scout would date a blue general?
jokes.setup: Criticizing the failed Stratego movie
jokes.punchline: Treating board game piece relationships as serious character development
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor that parodies both board game adaptations and Hollywood's obsession with romance subplots.

jokes.text: brett ratner had final cut.
jokes.setup: Explaining why the Stratego movie failed
jokes.punchline: Blaming the notoriously criticized director
jokes.explanation: Mild industry joke that requires knowledge of Brett Ratner's reputation, somewhat inside.

jokes.text: It is not money I am after, or women-- I know that is impossible.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy explaining his motivations to executives
jokes.punchline: Casually acknowledging his romantic hopelessness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Character moment - Comic Book Guy's self-awareness delivered with his characteristic matter-of-fact pompousness.

jokes.text: No can do. Thank you. But we never give... Thank you. They'll laugh us out of hollywood. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Executives rejecting Comic Book Guy's demand
jokes.punchline: Comic Book Guy's persistent 'thank you' responses wearing them down
jokes.explanation: Great escalating gag that shows Comic Book Guy's unexpected negotiation skills through sheer persistence.

jokes.text: I played hardball with hollywood. The closest I will ever come to playing a sport in my life.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy boasting about his negotiation success
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging this metaphorical sport is his only athletic achievement
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy that perfectly captures Comic Book Guy's self-awareness and physical limitations through wordplay.

jokes.text: Do you know where you can buy ink for an amiga brand fax machine?
jokes.setup: Homer auditioning for the movie role
jokes.punchline: Asking about obsolete technology during his audition
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's complete misunderstanding of audition protocol.

jokes.text: You want to see it any other way? Like sad... Or brooklyn, or richard nixon voice?
jokes.setup: Homer being told he didn't get the part
jokes.punchline: Offering random alternative interpretations including Nixon voice
jokes.explanation: Good Homer desperation comedy with specific absurd choices that feel authentic to the character.

jokes.text: But I already bought a boat with the money you were gonna pay me.
jokes.setup: Homer being rejected for the role
jokes.punchline: Already spending money he never had on something impractical
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - counting chickens before they hatch and choosing the most impractical purchase.

jokes.text: We need a dumpy, unappealing loser. -- an everyman
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy explaining what they need for the role
jokes.punchline: The brutal honesty about wanting an unappealing protagonist
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary on casting and the concept of 'everyman' delivered with Comic Book Guy's characteristic bluntness.

jokes.text: Can you help me out? I need change for a dollar. Oh, I also need a dollar.
jokes.setup: Homer asking for change
jokes.punchline: Revealing he doesn't even have the dollar to change
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup/punchline that perfectly captures Homer's financial ineptitude and circular thinking.

jokes.text: Great. Do you want to see me naked? Oh, there's no nudity in this movie. What movie?
jokes.setup: Homer being told he got the part
jokes.punchline: Immediately offering nudity then realizing he doesn't know what movie
jokes.explanation: Good escalating sequence showing Homer's eagerness and complete lack of context awareness.

jokes.text: Great. Marge, go. Guess who the garden club Elected president? You? Awesome. Next. No. I lost. Eh, they can go to hell. Bart?
jokes.setup: Homer asking family to share good news
jokes.punchline: His dismissive reaction when Marge didn't win and immediate move to next person
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer selfishness - only interested in good news and immediately hostile to anyone who didn't succeed.

jokes.text: Fine. I'll give back the oscars I stole from the lobby.
jokes.setup: Being told they have a problem
jokes.punchline: Immediately confessing to a completely different crime
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer guilty conscience leading to confession of unrelated crime

jokes.text: Several people stared at the sun, Attempting to blind themselves. Against every animal instinct they had.
jokes.setup: Explaining negative test audience reactions to Homer's photo
jokes.punchline: People choosing self-harm over looking at Homer
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark escalation that takes the rejection to absurd extremes with specific visceral imagery.

jokes.text: I'm a yo-yo dieter: 'yo, hot dog man. Yo, peanuts.'
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his diet struggles
jokes.punchline: Misunderstanding 'yo-yo' as literally saying 'yo' to food vendors
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that perfectly captures Homer's literal thinking and food obsession. Classic Simpsons humor.

jokes.text: Why aren't you laughing? Don't you know what 'yo' means?
jokes.setup: Homer's yo-yo diet pun falling flat
jokes.punchline: Homer thinking the problem is the audience doesn't understand slang
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Homer's obliviousness to why his joke didn't land.

jokes.text: Why aren't you laughing? Don't you know what "yo" means?
jokes.setup: Homer's failed yo-yo dieter joke
jokes.punchline: Assuming others don't understand rather than recognizing his joke failed
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up showing Homer's inability to read the room

jokes.text: They look good with a little meat on their bones.
jokes.setup: Presumably seeing unflattering before photos
jokes.punchline: Homer preferring the 'before' versions
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's contrarian taste and preference for fuller figures.

jokes.text: ...A lot of people have come to this town and changed, But not me. Now excuse me, I have to read a story To my adopted cambodian orphan.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy claiming he hasn't changed in Hollywood
jokes.punchline: Immediately revealing an extremely Hollywood lifestyle change
jokes.explanation: Excellent irony showing Comic Book Guy's complete transformation through immediate contradiction of his claim.

jokes.text: Hello, prius-- give daddy a kiss.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy mentioning his adopted orphan
jokes.punchline: The child being named after a car
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that adds specific Hollywood absurdity with the environmentally conscious car name.

jokes.text: Because I'm swallowing my frustrations and disappointments?
jokes.setup: Being asked why he eats
jokes.punchline: Homer showing unexpected psychological insight
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Homer's rare moments of self-awareness.

jokes.text: We'll do it in a montage to the song 'eye of the tiger.' Oh, that song's a little on the nose. Can we do it to david bowie's 'heroes'?
jokes.setup: Trainer suggesting a training montage
jokes.punchline: Homer critiquing the musical choice as too obvious
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about movie tropes with Homer showing unexpected sophistication about clichés.

jokes.text: How about I just do a walking around sad montage?
jokes.setup: Homer trying to avoid the difficult training montage
jokes.punchline: Suggesting the laziest possible alternative
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer laziness - trying to find the easiest possible version of effort.

jokes.text: Then I get out of the truck and go to thank the driver, But then I see the driver is a chicken?
jokes.setup: Homer pitching increasingly elaborate montage ideas
jokes.punchline: The completely random chicken driver twist
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor showing Homer's imagination running wild with random elements.

jokes.text: Tobey! Cider house rules! Total wuss. Seabiscuit, you keeping off the sugar cubes?
jokes.setup: Trainer taking a phone call
jokes.punchline: Insulting and giving dietary advice to Tobey Maguire
jokes.explanation: Standard celebrity trainer stereotype with mild movie references and horse/actor confusion.

jokes.text: No, no, no, no, no, don't worry. He totally sucks. I'm only lying to him.
jokes.setup: Trainer reassuring someone on the phone about Homer
jokes.punchline: Admitting he's deceiving Homer while Homer can hear
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing the trainer's duplicitous nature and professional callousness.

jokes.text: Hey, home town buffet, like I told john travolta, my client list is private.
jokes.setup: Homer asking if that was Tobey Maguire
jokes.punchline: Claiming privacy while immediately name-dropping John Travolta
jokes.explanation: Good contradiction showing trainer's hypocrisy about client confidentiality

jokes.text: Hit the pole! Stripper up...Fireman down.
jokes.setup: Training Homer on a pole
jokes.punchline: Using stripper terminology for firefighter training
jokes.explanation: Mild double entendre that plays on pole-related activities but feels somewhat forced.

jokes.text: And thank wonder woman for giving me the boobs to distract the guards.
jokes.setup: Everyman explaining how he saved the day
jokes.punchline: Crediting Wonder Woman's powers for giving him breasts
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor that takes the power-absorption concept to unexpected body transformation territory.

jokes.text: That's the problem when you have 17 writers.
jokes.setup: Kids noting plot holes in the movie
jokes.punchline: Blaming the excessive number of writers
jokes.explanation: Standard Hollywood joke about too many writers causing script problems.

jokes.text: Oh! There's less of you to love, But I love you even more!
jokes.setup: Marge seeing Homer's improved physique
jokes.punchline: The paradoxical increase in love despite less physical Homer
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment that captures Marge's unconditional love through logical contradiction.

jokes.text: Aah! What was that? Your other hand.
jokes.setup: Marge being affectionate with fit Homer
jokes.punchline: Being surprised by his newly toned body
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy about Marge adjusting to Homer's transformation.

jokes.text: I smell oscar. Oscar, take a shower, or a tomato bath or something. Please!
jokes.setup: Executive saying he smells Oscar (award)
jokes.punchline: Literally addressing a smelly person named Oscar
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that subverts the expected Hollywood award reference with literal interpretation.

jokes.text: At least you get to see my face-- unlike carl.
jokes.setup: Homer saying he gets to hire his friends
jokes.punchline: Carl being hired but kept hidden from view
jokes.explanation: Mild visual gag about the hierarchy of Homer's friends in his movie success.

jokes.text: A lot of things come and go in life, But not fame and fitness.
jokes.setup: Homer philosophizing about his success
jokes.punchline: The naive belief that fame and fitness are permanent
jokes.explanation: Good irony that sets up Homer's inevitable fall by showing his hubris about temporary things.

jokes.text: Don't take it personally, home team.
jokes.setup: Trainer leaving Homer
jokes.punchline: Using 'home team' instead of Homer's name
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay that shows the trainer's impersonal approach to clients.

jokes.text: Just remember what I taught you. But you taught me the only people who can stay thin Are those who can afford $5,000-a-week nutritional consultants!
jokes.setup: Trainer giving parting advice
jokes.punchline: Homer revealing the trainer taught him expensive dependency, not self-reliance
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy about the fitness industry's business model of creating dependency rather than independence.

jokes.text: Ooh! This other cheese cube stuck to the first one. Well, that still technically counts as one.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to stick to his diet
jokes.punchline: Using technicalities to justify eating more
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - finding loopholes in his own diet restrictions through creative interpretation.

jokes.text: Mm, it's bad luck to eat an uneven number of cheese cubes.
jokes.setup: Homer having eaten cheese cubes
jokes.punchline: Inventing superstition to justify eating more
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Homer's self-deception, moving from technicality to invented superstition.

jokes.text: The star's overweight, we're way over budget, And the grasshopperaptor we built Seems to have developed an evil mind of its own.
jokes.setup: Executive listing movie problems
jokes.punchline: The mechanical creature becoming sentient and evil
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that moves from mundane production problems to sci-fi horror territory.

jokes.text: I think you may have put on a little weight. Nag, nag, nag!
jokes.setup: Marge gently noting Homer's weight gain
jokes.punchline: Homer's disproportionate reaction to valid concern
jokes.explanation: Standard marital dynamic joke showing Homer's defensiveness about his weight.

jokes.text: Ah, here we are. A delightful buttermilk from 1961.
jokes.setup: Apu finding something to make Homer vomit
jokes.punchline: Offering decades-old dairy as if it's a fine wine
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Apu's bizarre inventory and treating expired food like vintage wine.

jokes.text: Oh, '61-- everyone was twisting again like they did the previous summer.
jokes.setup: Learning the milk is from 1961
jokes.punchline: Homer's nostalgic cultural reference to the Twist dance craze
jokes.explanation: Mild nostalgia humor that shows Homer's random cultural knowledge but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: This film is an embarrassment of jar-jarrian magnitude!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy criticizing the movie
jokes.punchline: Using Jar Jar Binks as the ultimate measure of embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy reference that captures his pedantic nature and creates a memorable insult scale.

jokes.text: Hollywood ponytail yank!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy fighting the executive
jokes.punchline: His specific fighting move targeting Hollywood executive hair
jokes.explanation: Excellent specific fighting move that perfectly targets Comic Book Guy's contempt for Hollywood stereotypes.

jokes.text: I produced bad summer movie, The parody of bad summer movies that was itself a bad summer movie.
jokes.setup: Producer establishing his credentials
jokes.punchline: The recursive failure of making a bad parody of bad movies
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on Hollywood's tendency to parody itself into the same problems it mocks.

jokes.text: Half the shots he's fat, half the shots he's thin--It's taking me out of the moment. Like when you're kissing a girl, and she burps.
jokes.setup: Criticizing the movie's continuity errors
jokes.punchline: The specific analogy to romantic moment disruption
jokes.explanation: Decent analogy that captures how continuity errors break immersion, though the comparison is mildly crude.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, now those kids have an embarrassment for a mother.
jokes.setup: Continuity supervisor explaining her situation
jokes.punchline: Executive's cruel response about her family
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that shows Hollywood cruelty but feels unnecessarily harsh without enough comedic payoff.

jokes.text: You went from everyman to everywhereman.
jokes.setup: Trainer commenting on Homer's weight gain
jokes.punchline: The wordplay suggesting Homer is now everywhere (fat)
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay that cleverly captures Homer's expanded size through the superhero name format.

jokes.text: Okay! It was turtle from entourage. I knew it!
jokes.setup: Homer demanding to know who the trainer left him for
jokes.punchline: The trainer admitting to leaving for a minor TV character
jokes.explanation: Mild celebrity culture joke that plays on the absurdity of leaving Homer for a fictional character.

jokes.text: He is a great guy. He's an awful guy. You just said both those things to me. I'm sorry. I'm not sorry.
jokes.setup: Trainer giving contradictory statements about Turtle
jokes.punchline: The continued contradictions about his apology
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the trainer's inability to maintain consistent positions, reflecting his superficial nature.

jokes.text: Yeah, the projectionist hung himself at the final reel.
jokes.setup: Acknowledging the movie isn't great
jokes.punchline: The extreme reaction of someone who had to watch it professionally
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation that effectively communicates the movie's awfulness through extreme professional reaction.

jokes.text: If there is one fault to find with this $200 million production, It is that this is the worst movie ever!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy seemingly offering balanced criticism
jokes.punchline: The devastating review delivered as if it's minor criticism
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that sets up diplomatic criticism before delivering absolute condemnation. Perfect Comic Book Guy pedantry.

jokes.text: I'm so cold.
jokes.setup: Homer alone after his movie career ends
jokes.punchline: The simple, pathetic complaint that undercuts any dignity
jokes.explanation: Simple ending that captures Homer's return to mundane reality after his brief fame.

tmdb_overview: Homer gets the lead in a movie about a superhero who can take on the powers of every superhero in any comic book he touches. To whip Homer into superhero shape, the studio hires a celebrity fitness trainer.
